trajectory_summary: The call moves cleanly through all structural beats and ends with a booked Tuesday 6PM appointment. The rep is structurally competent — verify completed, bill data captured, scheduling handled efficiently. What the call lacks is any energy amplification: the warm open with neighbor social proof and a genuinely engaged customer was never leveraged into emotional alignment, the bill was captured as data and immediately dropped, and the wife on title was identified but never included in the appointment. The call books, but it books at the floor of what this lead's quality warranted.
outcome_dispute: v1 outcome tag is unknown. Based on the transcript, the call ends with a booked phone appointment for Tuesday at 6PM. Outcome should be tagged 'appointment_booked'. The appointment has a structural vulnerability: wife is on title but not confirmed on the call, which per protocols.spouse_decision makes this a conditionally fragile booking.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Spouse-protocol completion — from ask to inclusion
primary_coaching_focus.quote: My wife and I are on the title.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The rep asked the right question and got the right answer, then did nothing with it. The wife is on title and not confirmed on the Tuesday appointment. This is the single most actionable structural fix on this call — it's a one-sentence move ('What time works for both of you?') that protects the sit. Manager should use this moment to distinguish between asking the protocol question and completing the protocol action.

key_moments.stewart_read: Customer volunteers that his neighbor used Ion — the single strongest legitimacy and social-proof signal a setter can receive. The rep's response is a flat acknowledgment and a pivot to asking the neighbor's name. He never anchors on what this means: the customer already trusts Ion, already has a reference point, and is predisposed to book. This was the moment to say something like 'That's great — what did your neighbor tell you about the experience?' and let the customer sell himself. Instead the rep treated it as a data point and moved on.

key_moments.quote: My wife and I are on the title.
key_moments.stewart_read: Rep correctly asks the title question at 01:32 and gets confirmation that the wife is on title. He then never returns to this fact. The appointment is booked for Carter alone — there is no 'what time works for both of you' or any attempt to confirm the wife will be on the Tuesday 6PM call. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the correct move is to include the spouse in the appointment, not to treat the title question as a checkbox.

Rep captures average kWh usage of 846 and a bill range of $5.24–$6.99 (likely hundreds, given context). His response is 'Cool' followed immediately by a scheduling pivot. Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, capture alone is necessary but not sufficient — the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. The flip was available: 'So you're paying [X] every month to Dominion — that's the number we're working to eliminate.' Instead the rep treated the bill as a form field and moved on.

key_moments.quote: And then once you find it, I sent you a email. So you should be able to send it there. What we're looking for is the COBOL usage. So you could pull up the PDF version of the bill, or there's also a part that just shows the usage, I think, on Dominion's website. Either way, it works.
key_moments.stewart_read: There is approximately 60 seconds of dead air between 02:30 and 03:47 while the customer navigates the Dominion website. During this window the rep directs the customer to send the bill to an email address. This is the anti-pattern flagged in codex bill_collection.stewart_automated_bill_capture: rep is routing bill collection through a personal or untracked email channel rather than a systematic capture path. The rep also says 'I think' when describing Dominion's website layout — a minor knowledge-gap signal on a utility that is presumably common in Ion's service area.
